name: Cait Sidhe
description: The Cait Sidhe, also known as the Fairy Cat, is a creature from Celtic mythology, particularly Scottish and Irish folklore. It is depicted as a large, spectral black cat with a white spot on its chest. The Cait Sidhe is not merely a house cat grown large; it is a magical being, possessing intelligence and supernatural abilities. They are often associated with witchcraft and are believed to steal the souls of the dead if their bodies are not watched over carefully before burial. Legend states that they can only be killed by silver. They are also believed to bring blessings to those who treat them well, and curses to those who offend them. On Samhain (Halloween), people would leave out saucers of milk for the Cait Sidhe, hoping for good fortune. While often portrayed as mischievous, they are also seen as guardians and protectors, particularly of those who are kind to animals. The Cait Sidhe is a powerful symbol of the Otherworld and the magical undercurrents that flow through the natural world.
subtitle: The Spectral Fairy Cat of Celtic Lore
appearance: The Cait Sidhe typically appears as a large, jet-black cat, often described as being the size of a dog. A distinguishing feature is a single white spot on its chest. Its eyes are often described as glowing with an otherworldly light, and its presence is said to emanate an aura of magic and mystery. Some accounts describe them as having exceptionally long tails.
habitat: Cait Sidhe are typically found in the remote woodlands and hills of Scotland and Ireland. They are often associated with ancient ruins, standing stones, and other places where the veil between the mortal world and the Otherworld is thin. They may also be found near human settlements, particularly those who are respectful of nature.
behavior: The Cait Sidhe are said to be intelligent and cunning creatures. They are often mischievous and playful, but can also be fiercely protective of their territory and those they deem worthy of their protection. They are said to be fond of milk and can be lured with offerings of it. Offending a Cait Sidhe is believed to bring bad luck. They are solitary creatures for the most part, but are said to gather in large numbers on Samhain.

keyMyths.title: Samhain Offerings
keyMyths.description: On Samhain, people would leave out saucers of milk for the Cait Sidhe in hopes of receiving blessings and good fortune throughout the year. Failing to do so could result in misfortune brought on by the angered fairy cat.

keyMyths.title: Soul Stealing
keyMyths.description: It was believed that Cait Sidhe could steal the souls of the dead if their bodies were not guarded carefully before burial, especially if a cat was allowed to pass over the corpse. Vigils were held to keep cats away.

sources.author: W.Y. Evans Wentz
sources.description: A classic study of Celtic folklore and beliefs about fairies.
sources.text: A classic study of Celtic folklore and beliefs about fairies.

sources.author: Lady Wilde
